worth_it_explanation: Budget and pacing problems are among the few campaign-level issues that can drain an entire flight's spend without an obvious signal in the wrong direction. A pacing type that does not fit the flight exhausts budget in the first days and leaves nothing for the back half, or it underspends at the end and returns money the campaign was supposed to put to work. The order-to-line-item budget relationship is a common setup mistake that causes one line item to absorb the full order budget while others effectively go dark. These checks take minutes to run in the Amazon DSP console and the findings are either a quick setting change or a clear recommendation to restructure the order.
intro: Amazon DSP delivers budget at two levels: the order and the line item. The order holds the total flight budget and the overall flight dates. Each line item within the order carries its own budget cap and its own pacing setting. The pacing setting controls how the line item distributes its budget across the flight: 'ASAP' spends as fast as inventory allows, while 'Even' spreads spend across the flight at a roughly uniform daily rate. Both settings exist because different campaign goals call for different delivery shapes. A promotional flight with a hard end date and a brand-awareness goal needs even delivery so the message runs across the full window. A product launch with uncapped inventory and a reach objective may benefit from ASAP delivery early in the flight. These checks confirm that the pacing setting matches the actual campaign goal, that order and line item budgets are set up in a way that lets all line items spend, and that flight-end underpacing does not leave significant budget unspent.

audit_checks.title: Pacing type does not match the campaign goal and flight structure
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A pacing type that conflicts with the campaign goal changes the delivery shape of the flight. ASAP pacing on a brand-awareness order pushes most impressions into the first days, so the message does not reach the audience across the full window the flight was designed for. Even pacing on a promotional order with a short window can leave budget unspent if inventory is thin on the scheduled days.

educate.base:
Amazon DSP gives each line item a pacing setting that determines how the line item distributes its budget over its flight. The two options are 'ASAP' and 'Even'. ASAP pacing spends as fast as eligible inventory clears the targeting and bid price, which means most of the budget can go out in the first portion of the flight. Even pacing uses a daily budget target derived from the total budget divided by the number of days remaining, so spend is distributed at a roughly consistent rate from the start of the flight to its end.

The right choice depends on what the campaign is trying to do. A flight built around brand awareness or reach depends on consistent exposure over time. Audiences need to encounter the message across the full window for frequency and recall to build. ASAP pacing on this type of order collapses that window, delivering most impressions in the early days and leaving the remainder of the flight with little or no delivery. Even pacing fits here because it holds back daily spend to protect the back half of the flight.

A flight built around a short promotional event, such as a product launch window or a sale period, may need ASAP pacing when inventory is plentiful and the goal is to reach as many eligible people as possible before the event ends. Even pacing on a flight this short can fail to spend the full budget if daily inventory is thinner than the system expects.

A few signs that the pacing setting does not fit the goal:
  • A long brand awareness or streaming TV (STV) flight is set to ASAP and front-loads most impressions in the first third of the flight
  • A short promotional flight is set to Even and consistently underspends its daily target against available inventory
  • Delivery reports show a steep decline in daily impressions after the first week of a multi-week Even flight
You may want to check the pacing setting against the campaign brief or business objective before the flight is too far along to correct course.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an endemic advertiser running always-on product campaigns, Even pacing protects spend continuity across the flight so that retail audiences encounter the ad throughout the consideration window, not just in the first days. A product detail page view retargeting campaign set to ASAP burns through retargeting reach quickly and leaves the audience unaddressed for the remainder of the flight.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a non-endemic advertiser running a brand campaign on streaming TV or display, Even pacing spreads impressions across the flight so frequency does not spike in the early days. If the campaign has a clear event anchor, such as a product announcement or a seasonal promotion, you may want to discuss whether ASAP delivery in the days around the anchor date is intentional or a default setting that was not reviewed.

investigate.intro: Review the pacing setting on each line item alongside the order's campaign goal, flight length, and delivery pattern to determine whether the setting fits the objective.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Open the Amazon DSP console and navigate to the order you are auditing. Note the flight start and end dates and the campaign objective if it is documented in the order name or notes.
Open each line item within the order and record its pacing setting (ASAP or Even).

Navigate to the line item's delivery report and review the daily impression or spend trend. A front-loaded trend on a multi-week flight under ASAP pacing, or a flat underdelivery trend on a short promotional flight under Even pacing, confirms a mismatch.

Cross-reference the pacing setting against the business goal described in the campaign brief or the objective documented in the order structure. Note each line item where the pacing type appears inconsistent with the goal.

amazon_marketing_cloud.label: Amazon Marketing Cloud (AMC)
steps: If Amazon Marketing Cloud (AMC) access is available, run a daily delivery query for the order's line items. Review the distribution of impressions and spend across the flight window.

A front-loaded distribution across a long flight under ASAP pacing confirms that most of the delivery occurred early. Compare the daily impression counts in the first week to the last week of the flight to quantify the imbalance.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per line item reviewed, the pacing setting, the flight length, the campaign goal, and the delivery trend observed. A line item whose pacing type fits the goal (e.g., Even on a multi-week awareness flight; ASAP on a short promotional burst with ample inventory) passes. A line item where the pacing type produces a delivery shape that conflicts with the campaign goal is a fail, recorded with the line item name, the pacing setting in use, and the delivery pattern observed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each line item's pacing setting, flight structure, campaign goal, and delivery trend above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more line items in {{site_name}}'s Amazon DSP campaigns are using a pacing setting that does not match the campaign goal or flight structure. The delivery shape the current setting produces either front-loads impressions in the early portion of the flight on a long awareness campaign, or holds back daily spend on a short promotional flight where inventory is available to clear. In both cases the budget does not work the way the campaign was designed to. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the pacing setting on each affected line item against the campaign objective and flight length, switching Even and ASAP settings where the mismatch is confirmed, and monitoring daily delivery trends in the Amazon DSP console for the first several days after the change to confirm the new delivery shape fits the goal.

audit_checks.title: Line item budget caps prevent the order from spending its full budget
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When the sum of all line item budget caps within an order falls below the order's total budget, the order cannot spend what it was allocated. The unused budget sits idle and the campaign goal does not receive the investment the flight was budgeted for. The reverse problem also occurs: a single line item with no cap can exhaust the order budget before other line items have a chance to deliver.

educate.base:
In Amazon DSP, the order holds the total budget for a flight and the line items within it compete for that budget. Each line item can carry its own budget cap that limits how much of the order budget it can consume, or it can run without a cap and draw as much of the order budget as its targeting and bids will allow.

Two budget-structure problems surface regularly during audits. The first is that all line items carry individual caps, and those caps sum to a total that is lower than the order budget. In this case the order budget can never be reached even if all line items deliver at their cap. The gap between the sum of the line item caps and the order budget represents money the account set aside for the flight but structurally prevented from spending. The second problem is the reverse: a single line item with no budget cap inside an order that contains multiple line items. Amazon DSP allocates order budget dynamically, and a high-performing line item with no cap can draw the majority of the order budget before the capped or lower-performing line items have a chance to deliver against their own goals.

A few signs that order and line item budgets are in conflict:
  • The order budget is set to a round number but daily delivery consistently falls short of the daily rate implied by that budget divided by the remaining flight days
  • One line item accounts for the majority of order spend while other line items in the same order show minimal delivery
  • The sum of all line item caps in an order, reviewed in the console, is noticeably lower than the order total
You may want to review the budget structure at the order level and the line item level together. The relationship between them is not always surfaced in a single view in the console.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an endemic advertiser running multiple targeting strategies within one order (e.g., an in-market audience line item alongside a retargeting line item), an uncapped retargeting line item can absorb most of the order budget if its audience is large and its bid clears inventory easily. You may want to confirm that each line item's budget cap reflects its intended share of the order, not just the absence of a cap.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a non-endemic advertiser running multiple ad formats within one order (e.g., a display line item alongside a streaming TV line item), the formats carry very different cost per thousand impressions (CPM) rates. A streaming TV line item with no cap can exhaust an order budget in days if the CPM is high and the targeting is broad. You may want to set explicit caps on the higher-CPM line items to protect the order's overall delivery balance.

investigate.intro: Review the order budget alongside the budget caps set on each line item to identify gaps where the structure prevents full spend or allows one line item to dominate.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Open the Amazon DSP console and navigate to the order you are auditing. Note the total order budget and the flight dates.
Open each line item within the order and record its budget cap, if one is set, or note if it is uncapped.
Add the individual line item caps together and compare the sum to the order budget. If the sum is lower than the order budget, note the gap.
Review the spend distribution across line items in the order's reporting view. Check whether one line item has consumed a disproportionate share of the order budget relative to the others.
Note any line items that are uncapped within an order where other line items are capped, as this creates an imbalance in how the order budget is distributed.

amazon_marketing_cloud.label: Amazon Marketing Cloud (AMC)
steps: If AMC access is available, run a query that returns total spend per line item for the order's flight window. Compare the distribution of spend across line items to the intended allocation.

A line item that accounts for more than its proportional share of the order budget, combined with other line items that stopped delivering before the flight ended, confirms a budget-structure imbalance.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the order budget, the sum of all line item caps within the order, and the spend distribution across line items. An order where the sum of line item caps equals or exceeds the order budget, and where no single uncapped line item dominates spend, passes. An order where the sum of line item caps falls below the order budget, or where one uncapped line item has absorbed a disproportionate share of the order budget at the expense of other line items, is a fail, recorded with the order name, the order budget, the sum of the line item caps, and the spend distribution observed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the order budget, each line item's budget cap, the sum of caps, and the spend distribution above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} a budget structure conflict in {{site_name}}'s Amazon DSP order setup. The sum of the line item budget caps within one or more orders falls below the order's total budget, which means the order cannot spend what it was allocated even if every line item delivers at its cap. In at least one order, a single uncapped line item has absorbed the majority of the order budget before other line items had the opportunity to deliver. In both cases the campaign's intended investment mix is not being executed. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing each order's line item caps and confirming that the sum of all caps meets or exceeds the order budget, setting explicit caps on high-CPM or broad-reach line items that could otherwise dominate the order budget, and checking the spend distribution across line items after any cap adjustments to confirm delivery is balanced as intended.

audit_checks.title: Budget underspend leaves a material portion of the flight allocation unspent at close
audit_checks.impact_rationale: An order that ends the flight with a significant portion of its budget unspent has not delivered the reach or frequency the campaign was planned for. The shortfall is not recoverable after the flight closes, and the client has effectively received less media than the budget was intended to buy.

educate.base:
A flight that closes with a meaningful portion of its budget unspent is a delivery failure, not a budget-efficiency win. The order was allocated a budget to purchase a volume of impressions across the flight window. When the flight ends under that volume, the audience received fewer exposures than the plan called for, and the advertiser paid for planning, creative production, and trafficking against a media goal that was not met.

Underspend can trace to several sources. Targeting that is too narrow leaves the line item with a small addressable audience that clears quickly and then delivers no further impressions. A bid that is too low relative to the floor on the inventory the line item targets means the line item rarely wins auctions. A frequency cap set so tightly that the addressable audience exhausts its allowed impressions early in the flight can also cap delivery before the budget is spent. Even pacing combined with a flight that launches on a low-traffic day can cause the daily delivery target to be set too conservatively, leaving the line item unable to catch up later in the flight.

A few signs of structural underspend:
  • The order's delivery report shows a consistent daily spend rate that falls below the implied daily budget (order budget divided by flight days) throughout the flight
  • The line item's delivery rate shows an early plateau followed by a long flat section before the flight closes
  • The frequency cap is reached by a share of the audience well before the flight ends, and the addressable pool is too small to add new users at the same rate
You may want to review the delivery trend early in the flight, not only at the end, so that pacing adjustments can be made while there is still time to recover delivery.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an endemic advertiser, underspend on a retargeting line item often traces to a frequency cap that exhausts the in-market audience before the flight ends. The segment refreshes as new shoppers enter and exit the consideration window, but if the cap is tight and the audience pool is small, new entrants do not replenish the addressable pool fast enough to sustain delivery.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a non-endemic advertiser running display or streaming TV at broad reach, underspend more often traces to a bid that is too low to clear the inventory the targeting requires. Display inventory on third-party exchanges has variable floor prices, and a bid set below the effective floor on the targeted placements will not win enough auctions to pace to the daily target.

investigate.intro: Review the order's delivery report and each line item's daily spend trend to determine whether the flight paced to its budget or fell short, and to identify the structural cause if it did.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Open the Amazon DSP console and navigate to the order. Compare total spend for the completed flight period against the order budget. Note the percentage of the budget that was spent.

Open the order's delivery report and review daily spend. Calculate the implied daily budget by dividing the order budget by the total number of flight days. Note whether daily spend consistently fell below that implied daily rate.
Open each line item and review its frequency cap setting and audience size estimate. Check whether a tight frequency cap combined with a small audience pool could account for the delivery shortfall.

Review the line item's bid settings. If the bid is set to a fixed CPM rather than a dynamic strategy, compare the bid against typical floor prices for the inventory type the line item targets. A fixed bid that is consistently below floor will not clear auctions.

amazon_marketing_cloud.label: Amazon Marketing Cloud (AMC)
steps: If AMC access is available, query the daily delivery for the flight and plot spend against the implied daily target. The pattern will indicate whether underspend was consistent from launch (a structural constraint such as a low bid or narrow audience) or developed mid-flight (a frequency cap that exhausted the available audience pool).

Query the reach and frequency distribution for the line item. If a share of the reached audience hit the frequency cap well before the flight ended, the frequency cap is contributing to the underspend.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the order budget, the total spend at flight close, the percentage of the budget delivered, and the most likely cause of the shortfall identified in the delivery report and line item settings. An order that spent 95% or more of its budget passes. An order that spent below 90% of its budget is a fail, recorded with the order name, the budget, the amount spent, the percentage unspent, and the likely cause. Orders between 90% and 95% are noted for monitoring on the next flight.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the order budget, total spend, percentage delivered, and likely cause of underspend above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more Amazon DSP flights in {{site_name}}'s account closed with a material portion of the budget unspent. The delivery reports show that daily spend consistently fell below the rate needed to exhaust the order budget by the flight end date, and the gap was not recovered before the flight closed. The planned volume of impressions was not delivered, so the audience received fewer exposures than the campaign was designed for. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the identified causes of the shortfall before the next flight launches: confirming the audience pool is large enough to sustain delivery through the full window, adjusting any frequency cap that exhausted the addressable audience before the flight ended, and reviewing the bid relative to typical floor prices on the targeted inventory to confirm the line item is clearing auctions at the intended rate.
